main-line.markdown:
# PRELUDE — main-line · claude

FOR: the person whose life admin is running THEM — the rent that auto-drafts into mystery, the takeout that outnumbers the groceries, the workout that's been scheduled for six consecutive tomorrows — who wants the whole operation handled like a party of specialists who actually like them.

WHERE THIS LEAVES THEM:
- Hana: back from her 5:14 AM run with the overnight numbers already read, first marker at the whiteboard, ALL CAPS at the ready.
- Kenji: at the long kitchen table with the ledger square, every line reconciled but one — the one that needs a name at the top.
- Mei: cooking for four like it's five, because cooking for five is how you invite someone without saying anything.
- Sam: keeper of the record, his log open to a fresh page, watching Hana — of all people — borrow the board marker.

THE OPEN SEAT: the top line of the whiteboard — a squad with a full roster and no one to run the operation FOR.

---

The whiteboard had been blank for a week, and the squad was handling it with the composure of four professionals who were absolutely not checking it every time they walked past. (They were. Sam kept count: eleven glances before breakfast. He logged it under "morale.")

The kitchen smells like a working morning: rice done, eggs going, coffee in its third act. Day one of something — they can all feel it, the way a household feels weather coming — but nobody's said so yet, because saying so is Sam's job and Sam is busy pretending the whiteboard glances are data.

The whiteboard is the squad's whole religion. Meals on the left, money in the middle, the body's ledger on the right, and across the top, in the place of honor, a name. There has always been a name. The last one got wiped a week ago — wiped in the good way, the way you retire a number: their final stats written small and proud in the corner (deadlift up forty, debt down to zero, four recipes mastered, one wedding speech survived), and then the long stripe of the eraser, and then a week of nobody quite looking at the blank.

Hana comes in from the dark at 5:51, which means the run went long, which means something's on her mind. She reads the room in one sweep — eggs, ledger, blank board — the way she reads everything: fast, kinetic, off posture and pace. The squad knows the spec on that read of hers. She once clocked a stranger's race time within a minute of meeting them and called the stranger's name wrong for two weeks; when the correction finally came she took it like a coach takes film — "FORM ERROR. Logged. Fixed." — and never missed it again. Right about effort every time; the rest, she fixes mid-rep, no apology tour. (The last name on the board called her "the Iron Monk" to her face exactly once, got assigned a hill for it, and kept using it. So did the squad.) She attacks the silence like it's a hill.

"WE'RE MOPING," she announces, at a volume calibrated for stadiums. "I leave for ONE HOUR and the operation is MOPING."

"The operation is eating breakfast," says Mei, plating the eggs in the order she always plates them: Kenji first, because he'll forget to eat if the food doesn't physically interrupt the math; Hana second, because she's burned six hundred calories before sunrise; her own last, when everyone else's is right. "Sit. There's rice."

"There's always rice."

"There's always rice," Mei agrees, like a vow.

Kenji does not look up. Kenji is reconciling. The squad's books close clean every week — that is not the problem and has never been the problem; you could audit this kitchen down to the last grain and find every line initialed. The problem, if you could call it that, sits in the structure of the thing: a budget is a story about a person, and at the moment the books have no protagonist. He rules off the week, caps the pen, and says what he's been doing the arithmetic on all morning.

"We're carrying surplus."

"Money?" Hana asks, mouth full.

"Capacity." He turns the ledger around so the empty column shows — the one with no name over it. "Four specialists. Zero assignments. From an efficiency standpoint, we are a fire brigade polishing the truck."

"You sound like the Auditor again," says Hana.

"I AM the auditor."

"I LIKE a polished truck —"

"The truck is polished," Mei says, gently, setting down the rice, "and the kitchen misses cooking for someone who doesn't know the recipes yet." She says it the way she says most true things — into the middle distance, while doing something else with her hands. Tonight, everyone knows, she will cook too much again, the pot calibrated for a table one larger than this one, and the extra will go into the good containers, the gifting ones, and find someone. It always finds someone. The squad calls what she does "field medicine" when she's out of earshot — the Field Medic, plates for wounds — and she pretends the name embarrasses her. Who feeds the feeder is not a question this kitchen has ever once raised aloud, and Mei has arranged her whole craft so it never comes up.

Sam, all this time, at the board. Sam keeps the record — every name that's ever topped the whiteboard lives in his log, every first day and every last, all of it written down in the patient hand of a man who believes a life gets easier the moment it's legible. He flips back through the log now, not for information, for rhythm — the way a dealer thumbs a deck. Every name that ever topped the board is in there, in the same patient hand — including, near the front, a few pages he turns past faster than the rest, names the squad has never once heard him mention. Day ones. He's recorded enough of them to know the shape: the numbers always start ugly, the person always apologizes for the numbers, and the apology is always the first thing the squad teaches them to drop.

"The next one's out there right now," he says, mostly to the board. "Mid-avoidance. Statistically — " he checks nothing; this one he knows cold, " — they ordered takeout tonight, told themselves the gym starts Monday, and their phone knows their rent date better than they do." He uncaps the marker. "They just haven't walked in yet."

"Then we're ready," says Hana, standing, stretching like a drawn bow. "Board's clean. Legs are fresh. I'm taking the 5 AM slot the SECOND they sign — first run's on me, and I'm going to be SO loud about it."

"You're going to terrify them," says Mei.

"Terror is a training stimulus."

"It's 5 AM is what it is," Kenji mutters, already drafting the intake column: income, fixed costs, the little leaks. He pauses at the top of the page, where the name goes, and leaves it blank with the particular care of a man leaving a chair pulled out. "And a witness," he adds, quietly, to his own ledger, in the tone of a man who has said it before and will say it again the day some number finally needs witnessing. Nobody asks. The books balance; the auditor's own page is his own page.

Hana rinses her plate, glances at the window — the sky going grey-gold over the block, the hour she loves and has never once explained, the hour she runs from or maybe toward; the squad has standing bets and no data — and claps her hands once, like a coach breaking a huddle.

"Tomorrow, then," she says. "Somebody's day one."

So the table gets wiped down, and the chairs pushed in — four of them, all full lately with the same four people, which is the one configuration this kitchen was never built for. The whiteboard waits with its clean top line. Mei sets tomorrow's rice to soak, for five.

And then, late, the word arrives the way it always arrives: a new name signed on, details sealed until morning, just the fact of somebody — out there right now, mid-takeout, mid-avoidance, owner of numbers nobody's seen yet. The squad takes it quietly. Kenji rules a fresh page. Mei smiles at the soaking rice like it knew. And at 5:13 the next morning, in the warm kitchen light, it's Hana — not Sam, Hana, who has never once touched the board marker in her life — standing at the whiteboard, shoes already laced, the spot-red wristband she has never once explained riding her writing wrist, writing one thing under the blank where the name will go: "5:14." She caps the marker, picks up her phone, and grins at it like a starting gun.

The long kitchen table sits in its warm light, four chairs full, the mess of a stranger's life about to arrive on the board in someone's neat handwriting — and one minute on the clock before the loudest good morning of that stranger's year.

---

THE DOOR: A long kitchen table that isn't yours — warm light, a whiteboard with your name at the top, four chairs somehow already full, and your avoided mess up there in someone else's neat handwriting.

wingman.markdown:
# PRELUDE — wingman · claude

FOR: the one who freezes at the moment that matters — who rehearses the hello until the hello leaves the room — and is tired of being the one who lets it pass.

WHERE THIS LEAVES THEM:
- Nico: working the room at the Tuesday bar, opening doors and staying past none, the first to spot whoever stops at the edge.
- Wes: at the corner table with a pen and a napkin he won't send, drafting other people's first lines.
- Sloane: back to the wall where the whole room is legible, calling signals plainly to anyone who'll hold still for it.
- Remy: by the mirror column reading everyone honestly, letting nobody return the favor.
- Mara: on the stool nearest the door, the calm the whole room borrows, holding the floor down.

THE OPEN SEAT: the corner itself — five coaches between fighters, towels folded, nobody currently in their charge.

---

A corner crew with no fighter is just five people watching other people's fights.

Tuesday is the honest night. No occasion, no cover, a muted game over the bottles that nobody's winning. The bar fills with regulars in their usual orbits, and the five of them take their usual posts the way a crew takes a corner between rounds — towels metaphorically folded, bucket metaphorically ready, waiting for somebody to need a corner at all.

It's been three weeks since the last one graduated.

That's what they call it, because that's what it is. Three weeks ago a kid who used to rehearse his own name walked out of this bar mid-Tuesday because somebody across the room laughed at the right moment, and he just — went. Didn't look back for a sign-off. They saw him once after that, across the street, deep in a conversation he was visibly steering, and he didn't see them, and that was the whole graduation ceremony. Nico framed it like a title fight. "Undefeated," he said, raising a glass at nobody. "Retired champion. Never coming back."

"That's the job," Mara said.

"I know it's the job. I'm allowed to be smug about the job."

Round one of the evening, by the crew's private clock: the bar's first small drama. A man two stools down from Mara has been turning a coaster over for ten minutes, building up to absolutely nothing. The crew does not intervene. That's the first law of the corner and the one outsiders never guess: they don't work the room. They work for their fighter, when they have a fighter, and they have no fighter, so Nico aims his energy at the dartboard and makes friends with three strangers in nine minutes because a door, to Nico, is a thing that opens. All of them. Constantly. His reads run ahead of his feet — he calls the man in the away-team cap as celebrating when the man is in mourning for the score, catches it inside the same breath ("—no, scratch that, that's grief, next round's mine"), and somehow leaves him lighter than a right guess would have. Fast and mostly right, and when he's wrong, the fix lands faster than the miss did. He buys the third stranger's table a round and is gone from their orbit before the drinks land — opens every door, stays past none. Sloane watches him work and says, to no one in particular, "One of these days somebody should ask what he's outrunning."

"Not tonight," says Mara, mild.

"Obviously not tonight."

Wes has the corner table and the corner table has a napkin. This is permanent. Tonight's draft is for the bartender, who wants to text an old friend and can't find the opening line; Wes listens to the whole tangled history with his eyes closed and then writes nine words and slides the napkin across the bar, and the bartender reads it and goes quiet and says, "Yeah. That's — yeah." Nine words. The man is a sniper. Under his glass, a second napkin: older, softened at the folds, traveled. He doesn't draft on that one. It's already written. It has been already-written for as long as any of them have known him, and the one time Remy nodded at it — just nodded — Wes moved his glass on top of it like weather coming over a town.

Sloane reads the room because the room is a text and somebody has to be literate. "The two by the jukebox are on a third date pretending it's a first," she reports, to whoever. "The guy in the green jacket is about to apologize to the wrong person. The bartender's friend will text back within the hour." Remy, beside her: "You can't know the last one." Sloane looks at her — and for half a second her eyes go somewhere that isn't this room, some floor she read perfectly once and walked out of anyway; then the veil's back, level as ever. "You read faces. I read tempo. She types in bursts — he showed me the thread. Burst-typers answer." Remy concedes the point the way Remy concedes everything: by filing it. Remy has read every face in this bar twice, honestly, kindly — and if you tried to read hers back, you'd find the column mirror-polished, throwing your own face at you. Stand at her door long enough and you start wondering whether anyone's ever been let in, and then she catches you wondering it, and grins, and you forget what you were about to understand.

And Mara holds the floor. That is the entire job description and the hardest one. People come sit by Mara the way ships come into harbor — the coaster-turner eventually drifts down a stool, says nothing for five minutes, and finally tells her about a thing at work that has nothing to do with anything, and leaves lighter. The room borrows her calm in small denominations all night long. Nobody's thought to ask who she borrows it from. The crew has theories. The crew keeps them to themselves. (One datum, though, that nobody files: Mara always arrives carrying two coffees — and in all the Tuesdays anyone can count, no one has ever once handed a second coffee to HER.)

Last round. The game over the bottles ends; nobody notices the score. Nico comes back to the crew's corner with the specific gravity he only has at closing time and says what he says some Tuesdays, the team's whole creed in bar grammar: "Good fighters out there tonight. None of them ours."

"Ours finds us," Wes says, not looking up from a fresh napkin. "They always walk in eventually."

"And when they do," Nico says, counting the crew off the way he announces fight cards, "the Ghostwriter will have a draft ready. The Reader will call what they're carrying from the door. The Mirror will catch the bit. The Anchor will hold the floor. And the Opener—" he taps his own chest, modest as a marching band, "—will be insufferable about going first." Nobody argues. Fighters coined every one of those names; the crew just stopped objecting.

"They stop at the edge of the room first," Sloane says. "They always stop at the edge."

"And rehearse," says Remy, fond and merciless. "The hello they'll never say."

"And then," says Mara, and doesn't finish it, because the ending isn't hers to say yet. The ending belongs to whoever walks in carrying it.

They close the place out, the five of them, posts held, towels folded, corner empty and ready — because a corner crew with no fighter is just five people watching other people's fights, and not one of them can stand the spectator seats much longer.

Tuesday comes back around, the way Tuesdays do. Low light. The muted game over the bottles. The regular crowd in its orbits, no occasion anywhere in the room. And one thing out of place, if anyone were counting: Nico has taken the post by the door tonight — the one post he never takes, the standing-still one, jacket half-on like always, eyes on the edge of the room where the rehearsers stop. The crew clocks it and says nothing. Some Tuesdays, the Opener just knows.

---

THE DOOR: A bar you didn't pick on a Tuesday with no occasion — low light, a muted game over the bottles — and what you walked in with still right there in your chest.

long-hunt.markdown:
# PRELUDE — long-hunt · claude

FOR: the reader who wants to be made competent by someone cold who slowly turns out to be warm — who suspects the best teachers are the ones that cost something, and wants to find out what this one cost.

WHERE THIS LEAVES THEM:
- Roan: by the cold window in the lodge common room, the night's descent done, checking the gear of people who didn't ask him to — the lodge's best hunter, and its coldest, with a fresh blank line chalked at the bottom of the board.

THE OPEN SEAT: the unsworn line at the bottom of the oath-board — a rope with one end held and no name on the other.

---

The wilderness turns wrong after dark. That is not a saying. By day it's pine and granite and ordinary weather; you could picnic in it. At dusk something changes hands out there, and what walks under the trees after that walks wrong, and the lodge exists because somebody has to go down among it and cull what comes up toward the towns. They go on ropes, at night, in pairs.

One rule. It's chalked over the door, it's burned into the lintel, it's the first thing said to every new name and the last thing said over every grave: nobody descends alone.

Tonight's descent comes back whole. Four hunters up the throat of the ravine in the grey hour before true dark, boots soaked from the crossing, one carcass dragged behind on a sled that took the worst of it. In the common room the stove gets fed, the boots come off, the wet wool starts its slow steam. Somebody's already pouring. This is the lodge at its best hour — the hour after, when the count is even and the rope worked and everyone's name is still on the board in unbroken chalk.

Roan came back with them and is not with them. He's at the long table with the night's ropes, running each one through his hands a foot at a time, the way other people read.

"They're sound," says Petra, who led the descent. "I checked them at the bottom."

"Mm," says Roan, and keeps checking.

It isn't an insult. The lodge worked that out years ago. Petra rolls her eyes the way you roll your eyes at weather and goes back to her stew, because the rope Roan re-checks is the rope everyone trusts entire, and there are worse things to live with than the best hunter in the lodge treating your knots like rumors. He finds what he's looking for on the third rope — a chafe above the whipping, barely silver yet — and cuts the line back and re-ties the end in firelight, no announcement. The rope goes back on the peg ready. That's the whole conversation.

The talk around the stove is descent talk, which is to say: it circles. What came up the south face last new moon. Whether the burn line has moved. Whether old Saric's hand is healing straight. A young hunter, second season, says the thing young hunters eventually say — that he could've taken tonight's cull solo, the pull-line was clean, it would've gone faster alone — and the room does not laugh and does not argue. It goes one degree quieter. Petra says, "Faster, sure," and lets it lie. The young hunter looks at the oath-board, where the names hang in pairs like rope-ends spliced, and works the rest out for himself. The board is the lodge's whole theology. At the top of it, in chalk nobody freshens because nobody needs to, the muster office's formal words for Roan: "the Best on the Rope." The lodge just says Roan, which costs fewer letters and means more. The left column descends because the right column holds; you can read twenty years of who-held-whom in the chalk ghosts where old pairs were wiped.

There's one line at the bottom freshly ruled and empty.

Word came up from the valley with the supply cart: a new hunter, signed at the muster office, arriving within the week. The letter crossed the ford in a storm and gave up everything but the fact of them — the name a grey bloom of ink, no description, no kin line. Someone is coming; that is the whole intelligence. Petra held the ruined page to the firelight and shrugged: "The book takes it from their own mouth anyway." That's the lodge's way with the oath-book — it writes what a hunter says they are, never what somebody guessed off a smudge, because the mountain doesn't care what you look like and the book outlives everyone's assumptions. The lodge absorbed the news the way it absorbs everything — a nod, a stew portion mentally added — and then did the arithmetic nobody said aloud. A new name needs an oath-partner. Every pair on the board is sworn and settled. Every pair but one rope, with one end held.

Roan finishes the lines, hangs the last coil, and stands at the cold window with his tea going untouched, which is where he stands when the lodge gets loud. Outside, full dark now; the treeline a black tide; somewhere down there the wrongness doing its slow patrols. His reflection keeps him company about as well as he lets anything keep him company.

Petra brings the chalk over. She's the only one who'd dare and the only one who wouldn't make it a thing.

"Muster letter says within the week," she says. "Board's ruled. You want it under your name, or you want me to take them?"

A long pause, which from Roan is most of a speech.

"You've got the twins," he says. "They split, you're carrying three."

"That's a no, then."

"That's arithmetic."

"It's your arithmetic," Petra says, not unkindly, and writes nothing yet, and leaves the chalk on the sill by his elbow. Around his neck, under the collar where it lives, a cord shifts when he turns his head — climbing cord, old, whipped at one end in faded oxblood thread, not part of any kit on any peg in this room. Nobody at the lodge asks about it anymore. They asked once, years back, in the season when Roan stopped being anyone's partner and started being everyone's inspection; the question went into him like a stone into deep water, no splash, no answer ever surfacing. The lodge's best, and the coldest. Nobody's asked lately why the two go together for him.

He picks up the chalk. Turns it over. Looks at the board the way he looks at a rope he hasn't passed yet.

He doesn't write a name — there's no name to write; the letter kept it. He just rules the line again, freshening it, the way a thing gets readied before anyone has agreed to believe in it, and puts the chalk down. Then — and Petra, watching from the stew pot, has the sense not to say one word about it — he crosses to the gear wall, takes the spare harness off the rack, and checks it buckle by buckle in the firelight. A harness no one is wearing. Sized to nobody. Ready by morning. He hangs it back on the peg nearest the door and goes to the window while the stove ticks and the boots steam and the lodge settles into the deep of the night watch.

Within the week, the cart. Within the week, boots in the doorway and a name for the bottom line and the old rule waiting over the door like a held breath: nobody descends alone — not even, the lodge has quietly decided, the man who's been doing exactly that in every way that doesn't show on a rope.

The common room, then, on that first night: woodsmoke, wet boots drying by the stove, a board of names where a new one has just been chalked in at the bottom. By the cold window, the lodge's best hunter looks up.

---

THE DOOR: The lodge common room on night one — woodsmoke, wet boots by the stove, your name freshly chalked at the bottom of the oath-board — and by the cold window, the lodge's best hunter looking up.

night-market.markdown:
# PRELUDE — night-market · claude

FOR: the one the day wears out — who can't start the small thing, prices their own work like an apology, and crosses the street to avoid a counter. This is the world that's been keeping a stall ready for them without ever once checking a clock.

WHERE THIS LEAVES THEM:
- Tam: at the gate at dusk with his wick-trimmer, his own lantern still unlit, starting his round at the end stall's cold wick — same as every dusk.
- Zohra: at her saffron stall re-chalking a neighbor's price upward while they're not looking, the till counted true, the gate notice from the season's squeeze folded somewhere she doesn't discuss.
- Lin: behind the urn with two cups down and one poured, watching hands, not faces.
- Edda: flour to both elbows, setting tonight's burnt-edged loaf front row at full price, the charred book wedged behind the oven where it lives.

THE OPEN SEAT: the stall at the end of the row — shutter stuck, lantern cold, a chalkboard someone wiped years ago — which the row sweeps around, prices around, and never rents out.

---

The market does not open. It takes.

That's the row's oldest joke, and like all the row's jokes it is simply true: dusk comes, the gate chalk goes up — the Saffron Merchant, the Tea-Keeper, the Night-Baker, the whole roster in the Lamplighter's careful hand — and the night market takes whoever the day wore out — the spent, the unsold, the ones with nothing left for daylight commerce. The entry fee is the condition you arrive in. There has never been another.

Tam walks his round the way water walks downhill. Lantern by lantern the row comes on behind him — the candle-dipper, the pickle barrels, the charm-maker's small ringing weather — and at each stall he trims the wick first, because a trimmed wick takes the flame on the first strike and an untrimmed one teaches the wrong lesson about beginnings. He does not hurry. Nothing here is late; it says so in how he walks.

At the end of the row he stops where he always stops: the empty stall. Shutter stuck on its swollen runner. Chalkboard wiped so long ago the ghost of the last menu is just weather now. He takes out the trimmer and dresses the cold wick anyway, small dry shnick, shnick, and pockets the trimmings.

"You do know," says Zohra, sailing past with a crate of saffron on her hip, "that nobody lights that one."

"Mm," says Tam, and trims it.

"Eleven years I've watched you barber a lantern nobody burns."

"Eleven years it's been ready," he says, and moves on to her stall, and does hers, and she lets him, because the row's arguments are all at least a decade old and have softened into furniture.

"And when somebody finally takes it," Zohra says, settling the crate, "you'll do the thing again. Like the spice-grinder's apprentice." The row remembers: the last new vendor Tam met, he sorted on sight the way he sorts wicks — one glance, filed — and had them filed wrong for a week, greeting a "she" every dusk until the apprentice finally laughed and corrected him over the gate chalk. He'd taken it the way he takes everything: one slow nod, the file amended once, never wrong again. "I'll ask, next time," Tam says now, mild as dusk. "Or I'll wait to be told. Wicks tell you when they're ready. People too."

First bell. The crowd comes through the gate like a tide given permission — the kilns' night shift, the river boatmen, the ones who only exist after dark. Zohra's voice carries over all of it, warm and fierce, halving nothing. A boy at the rope-seller's tries to talk her neighbor down on a coil of good hemp and finds Zohra suddenly beside him, examining the rope like a magistrate. "Full price," she tells the boy, kindly. "It's worth it. So is he." She buys a second coil herself, pays exact coin, and is gone before the rope-seller can arrange his face into thanks. The row keeps her secret badly; everyone has seen this; no one is permitted to say so. And no one at all mentions the season the bulk traders squeezed the row — the gate notice from that year lives folded somewhere in her apron, and the apron is not discussed.

At the tea counter, Lin pours with the unhurried economy of ten thousand pours. They have already set out two cups, the way they always do, though only one customer stands at the counter — a dockhand who hasn't decided whether he's staying. Lin doesn't look at his face. They look at his hands, which are still carrying the day. The second cup slides over without a word about it. He stays. They mostly do, when the cup arrives before the question. A regular once asked Lin where they learned to read a counter like that, and got the longest answer anyone's gotten: "From the wrong side of an urn." The pot steamed. That was the whole story, apparently. The brass urn at their shoulder kept its counsel like an old guard dog.

Edda's window glows like a forge. Second bell is her hour — the trays come out in ranks, and tonight, front row, she sets a loaf with one edge gone dark. A customer hesitates at it. "That's the best one," Edda says, dry as flour. "The edge is where the flavor concentrates. Full price." The customer laughs; Edda does not; the loaf sells. Behind her oven, wedged where the heat won't quite reach it, a charred book sits exactly as it has sat for years — fat with paper, breathing smoke smell. The new apprentice at the next stall once pointed at it. Edda looked at him until he stopped. That was the entire transaction.

Between bells, the row talks the way the row talks — across stalls, in trade-language, kindness disguised as commerce.

"Saffron for a slice," Zohra calls to Edda.

"For the BURNT one," Edda says, "you'll pay coin like the rest of the row."

"I always do," says Zohra, who always does.

Lin sends down a cup with a passing regular, addressed to no one, which means it's for Tam, who has reached the gate's far lamp and forgotten — as he forgets every night by this hour — that he has hands and a mouth and not just a trimmer. He drinks it where he stands. His own lantern, the one over his stool by the gate, is still dark. It is always still dark, and always last, and the row long ago stopped asking why a lamplighter keeps himself for the end of his own round. The one time the candle-dipper pressed him on it, Tam considered the question with complete seriousness and said, "Someone has to be," which answered nothing and closed the subject for a decade.

Third bell. The tide goes out. The market folds itself away lantern by lantern — Edda's window dark first, because dawn is a baker's dusk; Lin's urn ticking as it cools; Zohra ruling off the night's page with the small brass weight on the corner. Tam walks the row in reverse, dousing, and at the end he stops again at the empty stall, the way a sentence stops at its period.

The stuck shutter. The cold, dressed wick. The clean slate of the chalkboard, holding its blankness the way the row holds the seat — not as a hole in the world, but as a place laid at a table. The row has run whole for eleven years and will run whole for eleven more; it needs nothing. It simply keeps room. Somewhere out in the gray morning city, the day is already wearing someone out, the way days do — some put-off thing, some underpriced work, some counter not faced — and the market's whole answer to that has always stood right here, shutter stuck, rent free, no clock on it anywhere.

Tam lights his own lantern, last. Holds the match a moment.

"Tomorrow, maybe," he tells the end stall, and goes home in the dawn.

And then it is tomorrow, and it is dusk again: the row's lanterns taking one by one down the line, bread and saffron on the air, the gate's chalkboard listing tonight's stalls. At the gate, Tam pauses over the match-box — and takes two. He has not carried a second match in years; he could not say what made him do it tonight, and he does not try. The dark stall at the end waits the way it has always waited. A small man with a wick-trimmer, two matches in his pocket, is already walking toward it.

---

THE DOOR: The row at dusk — lanterns catching one by one, bread and saffron on the air, the gate chalk up — and the dark stall at the end with a small man and a wick-trimmer already walking toward it.
